item.title: What was destroyed
item.description: What many people don't know is that the destruction in Gaza wasn't “just” residential buildings, hospitals, schools, museums, and places of worship. No, every form of infrastructure for food production was also completely destroyed. No farmland, no fishing boats, no bakeries, nothing. Regardless of the context, that is simply pure evil.
item.dc:creator: IslamExplained

item.title: Letitia James Criminally Charged In Trump's Latest Effort To Punish Rivals – Bryan Stevenson:
item.description: "A number of prominent black women are targeted by Trump's ugly vindictiveness. Know what Racial Equality would look like in the USA? If James wrote 20 ALL CAP Social Media screeds at 3 AM, like Trump, in response – And it didn't count against her. She would never, she's not a manchild. It makes you think about humiliation, anger, white privilege, being black in America."
item.dc:creator: OceansRust

item.title: Suit Up! It's time to go to Antarctica
item.description:
In order to protect us from the elements, we are issued Extreme Cold Weather gear (ECW). We are allowed up to 85 lbs of checked luggage, but we have to make room within that weight for our ECW (estimated at about 15 lbs)
This board shows all the different ECW clothing that could be issued. Not everyone gets everything. A lot of the clothing assignment is job-related
We have to inventory our ECW bags to ensure that everything fits and that all the zippers and fasteners work. You wouldn't want to be out in the elements and not be able to close your jacket. First-year participants are required to take all issued gear. Whereas returning participants may opt out of certain items
ECW bags for a full flight
We're allowed up to 85 lbs of luggage to take South with us. We are instructed to make room within that 85 lbs to take our ECW with us (about 15 lbs)
Suited up and ready to go
May the spirit of Robert Falcon Scott guide us safely on our expedition
Now that we have our gear, let's go to The Ice. It's about a 5-hour flight to McMurdo from Christchurch, NZ
Made it safely. Here's a bunch of us pouring out of the C-17 and on to the Ross Ice Shelf at Phoenix Airfield. Wheeled aircraft need at least six feet of ice thickness in order to land. From here it's about an hour drive into "town" to get to McMurdo
Bronze Antarctic Sled Dog tax. Don't worry, he's a good boy. Not some kinda Thing!
item.dc:creator: VosperOfAntarctica

item.title: ICE ATTACK: "Officers" slam a 15 year old girl to the ground, then put his knee on her neck. This happened in Illinois.
item.description: Face it, MAGA, you're the bad guys in this story. History will remember you as evil pieces of shit. For everyone else, get ready and stay in groups. Resist. I can't help you from where I am other than being a messenger. Good luck, America.

item.dc:creator: deafkitten

item.title: *Excerpt stolen from FB*
item.description: “In 1952, inside a New York City delivery room, a baby was born blue and silent. Doctors hesitated, unsure whether to keep trying. Then a calm voice broke through the panic. “Let’s score the baby,” said Dr. Virginia Apgar. That moment changed medicine forever. Apgar had once dreamed of being a surgeon, but in the 1940s few women were allowed into the operating room. Told that no hospital would hire her, she turned to anesthesiology instead — a decision that would save millions of lives. Working in Columbia-Presbyterian’s maternity ward, she saw newborns die within minutes of birth because doctors had no system to judge which babies needed help first. So one morning in 1952, she grabbed a pen and paper and designed a five-point test measuring heart rate, breathing, muscle tone, reflex response, and skin color. She called it the Apgar Score. The idea spread faster than anyone expected. Within a decade, almost every hospital in America was using it. Infant mortality fell sharply. Doctors finally had a language for newborn care — and babies once thought lost were suddenly being saved. Apgar never stopped pushing forward. She earned a public health degree, joined the March of Dimes, and became a global voice for mothers and infants. When asked how she had thrived in a man’s world, she laughed, “Women are like tea bags — they don’t know how strong they are until they’re in hot water.” Dr. Virginia Apgar passed away in 1974, but her test still guides every delivery room on Earth. Every two seconds, somewhere in the world, a baby takes its first breath — and someone quietly calls out a number that honors the woman who refused to give up on newborns or on herself.” #VirginiaApgar #TheApgarScore -Nature Mind
item.dc:creator: malbec

item.title: Sigourney Weaver :)
item.description: Ellen Ripley in the movie #Alien (1979) was originally written to be a man. Director Ridley Scott changed his mind when he watched The Hunger Games (2012) and realized that women can also be action movie leads #Sigourney #Weaver

item.title: Happy birthday Claudia Black
item.description:
October 11th the Australian film, TV, and voice actress celebrates her 53rd birthday
Black has appeared in various genres, still her live action roles in franschises as Stargate and Farscape (above), Pitch Black, and Star Wars have cemented her as a sci-fi icon. In video games she has voiced characters in series like Mass Effect, God of War, Uncharted, Diablo, and Final Fantasy. Yet to me she above all is Morrigan, a Witch of the Wilds, ever since her introduction in Dragon Age: Origins (2009);
“Well, well, what have we here? Are you a vulture, I wonder? A scavenger poking amidst a corpse whose bones were long since cleaned? Or merely an intruder, come into these darkspawn-filled Wilds of mine in search of easy prey?”
item.dc:creator: Ngugi
